name: Gabbu Kathe
description:
Gabbu, the Garbage King, was born from human neglect and forgotten waste. One day, a sharp and curious ragpicker finds him.  
Through Kathak, contemporary movement, mime, humour and multimedia elements, this dance-drama balances satire with tenderness. Children and families are invited into a fantasy world in which discarded objects hold memories and tales of their own. At the end of the performance, children will be taught a short section of the choreography they witnessed.  
Waste is not the end of a cycle, but rather the beginning of a conversation around renewal, empathy and collective action.  
Credits:
Concept, Script, Design & Direction: Keerthi Kumar & Roopa Krishnamurthy
Original music score: Praveen D Rao & Sagar Gururaj
Artwork: Rohit Bhasi
Performed: Roopa, Keerthi Kumar, Bharat Rajan, Ashwin
Choreographic Supervision & Lighting Design: Yonitha Jain
Commissioned by Alif Arts as part of BLR Hubba
Produced by ARTMOS 

This event is a part of our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’. 


Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Illuminating Dance
description:
What transforms practice into performance? Production.  
The craft of lighting design is as nuanced as it is impactful. Colours, angles, intensity all influence the attention of the audience, the quality of the storytelling, and ultimately the experience of the entire performance.  
In this lecture-demonstration, acclaimed light designer Niranjan Gokhale will take us through the fundamentals of lighting design for dance. Using short performance clips as well as live demonstrations, you will witness how subtle changes can dramatically alter the reading of movement. He will take audience questions at the end of the session.  
For dancers, choreographers, and anyone curious about stagecraft, this session is an insight into the art that powers the spotlight.  
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Sound Judgment in an Age of Uncertainty
description:
Can wisdom be cultivated?  
We recognise it when we see it: an elder’s timely advice, a leader’s restraint, a friend who asks the right question at the right moment. But knowing wisdom is not the same as knowing how to become wise. 
Cognitive scientist Igor Grossmann argues that wisdom has two engines: cultural tools, such as proverbs, stories, and role models, that are transmitted across generations and learned over time, and the mental faculties behind sound judgement, which sharpen our use of cultural tools. The first one reminds us that no one becomes wise alone. This latter engine runs on self-distance: the ability to step back from our own thinking, sit with what you do not know, and hold competing perspectives before acting 
Wisdom is after all, a practice. Not merely a result of age or experience. 
The talk will then open to the audience for questions.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: What Holds
description:
A delicate balance between care and surrender.  
This performance showcases the quiet peace that emerges from the repetition of actions. Gayatri Shetty’s solo centres around arranging and rearranging blocks and the underlying grief that comes with the knowledge of their inevitable collapse.  
Playing with themes of labour and futility, performer and performance, this piece invites you to sit on three sides of the ‘stage’. You are given blocks of your own to engage with as you please. A mode of close witnessing, where time, presence, and attention become part of the choreography itself. What begins as a study of systems and precision gradually unravels into an intimate dialogue with loss. It becomes a collective experience, the tussle between patience and uncertainty.  
The ache of collapse, and the courage to rebuild. 
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Dāri | The Path
description:
A journey through time itself. 
When an ancient art form breathes within contemporary spaces, it is reborn. Kuchipudi, originating in the villages in Andhra Pradesh, has found new life with Avijit Das.  
This performance is an offering, a step in the unbroken rhythm of a tradition that has travelled across generations.  Through movement, music, and narrative, the performance reflects on how form, context, and audience have shaped Kuchipudi. 
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’. 

Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Fin-Verse: Building Money Skills
description:
What if learning about money felt less like a lesson and more like an adventure? 
In this interactive workshop, children explore how money works through games with real choices, working up to planning their very own vacation budget using play currency. Along the way, they solve mini challenges, make team decisions, and discover what it means to spend, save, and choose wisely. 
No finance talk. No cautionary tales. Only hands-on practice and the occasional ‘aha!’ moment making everything click. 
Smart money habits start early, and learning them can be fun! 
Around the World in 50 Sundays is a journey that begins, not with a ticket, but with opening a book. In a city like Bangalore where families are always in motion, the BIC Library hopes to offer stillness and a quiet, welcoming space for both parents and children where reading becomes a shared way of being and not a task. 
Every Sunday, we open our doors to invite you into a library that listens; building a rhythm of return, a habit of joy and a new community bound not just by books, but by the weekly ritual of reading together. 
We welcome your ideas and input. If you have suggestions for children’s events or activities that align with this vision, we’d love to hear from you. Please reach out to us at library@bangaloreinternationalcentre.org 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Jazz, Poetry and Songwriting
description:
Jazz is improvisation. Poetry is expression.  
Jazz poetry is the marriage of these two art forms. Running as a thread through the Harlem Renaissance, the Beat movement, and the Black Arts movement, it was born from the same impulse as the music itself: to feel something and refuse to keep it quiet. 
This evening begins with a performance of jazz poetry. Volunteers from the audience will then join the quartet to write and perform an original blues song—from scratch! 
Jazz the way it was intended to be experienced: up close and personal. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Resonance
description:
The veena from Ayodhya meets the bansuri from a Mumbai gurukul. The mridangam of a dynasty meets the tabla of the first generation. 
Some raagas on this programme belong to both Indian classical traditions, the same soul unfolding differently in each. Others belong exclusively to one. In the moments of improvisation, there are no boundaries between instrument and artist, North and South. 
An evening of Carnatic and Hindustani music, in conversation. Samvad.  
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Mothers of Daughters
description:
Language: English 
Idolised, worshipped, immortalised. A mother is asked to be everything while making it look effortless. 
Alas, a mother is also a daughter. In the face of her humanity, we know not what to do. 
Meyeder Mayera is a Bangla anthology of mothers written by their daughters. Not the idea of a mother, but the women themselves. Arundhati Ghosh joins four contributors to the book in a conversation about the legacies it carries, the relationships that shaped them, and what it means to truly see the person who raised you. 
The session will then open for audience questions.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Many Lives of Dance
description:
A movement practice is inherently living, breathing, and evolving. 
Dance is many things at once. A rigorous system of knowledge, shaped by centuries of technique, philosophy, and discipline. A language moving through communities, carried by rhythm, presence, and collective energy. A deeply personal practice, uniquely created by each mind, body and soul.  
The Many Lives of Dance is a week-long series in honour of World Dance Day, bringing together artists, scholars, and practitioners across forms, traditions, and contexts. From ancient rhythms to modern beats, the codified to the improvised, the performed to the participatory.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Thehraav
description:
To pause is political. To act with intention is radical.  
This talk explores the power of moving deliberately in an age of speed. Passivity and pause are often mistaken, and Masoom Parmar is here to set the record straight.  
Weaving the practical and the theoretical together, they unpack the nuances of the slow and intentional movements in dance. Invoking Indian temple and court movements, Parmar also brings in ethnographic and theological perspectives, to ask questions around whose bodies are allowed to pause, what attention does to the performer-audience relationship, and how inherited movement vocabularies carry cultural memory.  
They will take audience questions at the end.  
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Perspectives on Bharatanatyam
description:
Drawing from decades of performance and pedagogy, Leela Samson reflects on the evolving language of Bharatanatyam, tracing its journey from inherited tradition to contemporary inquiry. Through a combination of critical reflection and demonstration by dancer Bhavajan Kumar, she examines the form’s foundational principles while engaging with questions of interpretation, continuity, and change. 
The lecture demonstration will explore how Bharatanatyam’s vocabulary and expressive possibilities have been shaped over time, and how dancers today negotiate its historical frameworks alongside present day sensibilities. By interweaving practice with insight, the session offers audiences an understanding of how the form continues to transform while remaining rooted in its aesthetic and philosophical core. 
With this lecture demonstration, the BIC inaugurates our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: TechnoCats
description:
86 minutes | English with subtitles | 2026 
One million applicants. Seven seats. Then what? 
Seven young people from across India fight their way into IIT, only to find that getting in was the easier part. The filmmaker, an IIT alumnus himself, spent seven years with them, from their first semester to the third year of their careers. 
Decades after graduating, Gautam Sonti recognised in these students the same pressure he had once felt: a system that measures everything and nurtures very little. TechnoCats is his attempt at a conversation across generations. 
The screening will be followed by a discussion between Gautam Sonti, Tara Rao and Aunohita Mojumdar, before opening to audience questions. 
Watch the trailer here. 
In collaboration with:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Sole to Soul
description:
Hip-hop started as a revolution: moving the body in unexpected ways, breaking through oppression with expression.  
This workshop offers a gentle introduction to the foundations of hip-hop,  from cultural origins to footwork and rhythms. Beginning with a discussion on the history and origins of this style, the dancing that follows is contextualised. Participants will explore hip-hop rhythm and grooves, as well as elements of house dance.  
Playful and expressive, this event is for anyone who is interested in learning to dance without pressure. 
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate World Dance Day 2026, ‘The Many Lives of Dance’.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India
